readings.title: Reading 1
readings.text:
It happened that while Apollos was in Corinth, Paul made his way overland as far as Ephesus, where he found a number of disciples.
When he asked, 'Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you became believers?' they answered, 'No, we were never even told there was such a thing as a Holy Spirit.'
He asked, 'Then how were you baptised?' They replied, 'With John's baptism.'
Paul said, 'John's baptism was a baptism of repentance; but he insisted that the people should believe in the one who was to come after him -- namely Jesus.'
When they heard this, they were baptised in the name of the Lord Jesus,
and the moment Paul had laid hands on them the Holy Spirit came down on them, and they began to speak with tongues and to prophesy.
There were about twelve of these men in all.
He began by going to the synagogue, where he spoke out fearlessly and argued persuasively about the kingdom of God. He did this for three months,

exegesis.summary: Paul, arriving in Ephesus, discovers disciples who have received only John's baptism of repentance and have never even heard of the Holy Spirit. After teaching them that John pointed forward to Jesus, Paul baptizes them in the name of the Lord Jesus and lays hands upon them; the Spirit descends, and they prophesy and speak in tongues. The episode unveils the difference between the preparatory baptism of John and the full sacramental baptism of Christ, which alone confers the Holy Spirit and incorporates the believer into the Risen Lord.

patristicQuotes.quote: The baptism of Christ does not undo the baptism of John, but includes it in itself; he who is baptized in Christ's name has both baptisms, that of water and that of the Spirit. Thus the Apostle, having found certain Ephesians baptized with John's baptism, baptized them again in the name of Christ, because they had not been baptized in the Spirit; for as the baptism of Christ was more than that of John, it was a new one given, not the same repeated.

We baptize again after John's baptism; after a homicide's we baptize not: because John gave his own baptism, the homicide gives Christ's; which is so holy a sacrament, that not even a homicide's ministration can pollute it. It is by this power which Christ retains in His own possession exclusively that the unity of the Church is established.

cccReferences.title: John's baptism prepares; baptism in water and the Spirit gives new birth
Baptism in the name of Jesus brings the gift of the Holy Spirit
Baptism incorporates the believer into Christ's death and resurrection
Apostolic laying on of hands confers the Holy Spirit
The Spirit who rested on the Messiah is given through Christ's sacraments

exegesis.thematicConnection: Easter weekday readings prepare the Church for Pentecost. Acts shows the Spirit completing what John's baptism could only foreshadow; the Psalm sings of God arising in power among His people; the Gospel records Christ's pledge that, though He depart, the Father remains with Him and His victory will not abandon the scattered disciples. The Spirit poured out at Ephesus is the same Spirit Christ promises to send.

readings.title: Responsorial Psalm
readings.text:
You disperse them like smoke; as wax melts in the presence of a fire, so the wicked melt at the presence of God.
The upright rejoice in the presence of God, delighted and crying out for joy.
Sing to God, play music to his name, build a road for the Rider of the Clouds, rejoice in Yahweh, dance before him.
Father of orphans, defender of widows, such is God in his holy dwelling.
God gives the lonely a home to live in, leads prisoners out into prosperity, but rebels must live in the bare wastelands.
God, when you set out at the head of your people, when you strode over the desert,
readings.refrain: You disperse them like smoke; as wax melts in the presence of a fire, so the wicked melt at the presence of God.

exegesis.summary: Psalm 68 is a triumphal hymn celebrating God's arising in power: the wicked melt like wax before His face, but the righteous exult and dance. He is named Father of orphans, defender of widows, the One who brings the lonely into a home and prisoners into freedom. The Church places this royal Easter psalm on the lips of the disciples awaiting the Spirit, for the Risen Christ has ascended on high, leading captivity captive, and now establishes His dwelling among the poor and the abandoned.

patristicQuotes.quote: God ever appears in a cloud, according to the Psalms, 'clouds and darkness are round about Him.' Therefore shall the Son of Man come in the clouds as God and the Lord, not secretly, but in glory worthy of God, with great power and majesty.

The Word of God entered the ship, that is, the Church; the multitude follows the Lord out of the city into the desert, going from the Synagogue to the Church. The Lord sees them, has compassion upon them, and heals all sickness and infirmity, cleansing their obstructed minds and unbelieving hearts for the understanding of the new preaching.

cccReferences.title: The cloud both veils and reveals the saving presence of God
God's people learn to pray in the shadow of His dwelling
Prayer flourishes under the guidance of shepherds and prophets
The earthly liturgy is a foretaste of the heavenly
God draws scattered humanity into the unity of His family

exegesis.thematicConnection: The Psalm voices the joy of those whom God gathers and indwells — the very experience of the Ephesian twelve, who pass from being uninstructed strangers to Spirit-filled members of Christ's Body. It also answers the Gospel's warning of scattering: even when disciples scatter, God remains 'Father of orphans,' giving the lonely a home. The Spirit who rises within them is the answer to the Psalmist's plea.

readings.title: Gospel
readings.text:
His disciples said, 'Now you are speaking plainly and not using veiled language.
Now we see that you know everything and need not wait for questions to be put into words; because of this we believe that you came from God.'
Jesus answered them: Do you believe at last?
Listen; the time will come -- indeed it has come already -- when you are going to be scattered, each going his own way and leaving me alone. And yet I am not alone, because the Father is with me.
I have told you all this so that you may find peace in me. In the world you will have hardship, but be courageous: I have conquered the world.

exegesis.summary: At the close of the Last Supper Discourse the disciples claim, at last, to understand Jesus plainly and to believe He came from God. Christ replies with sober love: their belief is real but fragile, for the hour is upon them when they will be scattered, each fleeing his own way and leaving Him alone. Yet He is not alone, because the Father is with Him. He speaks all this so they may find peace in Him, for though the world brings tribulation, He has already conquered the world.

patristicQuotes.quote: When He says 'I am no more in the world,' He means that He was soon going away. As man He prays God for His disciples; as God He is one with Him to Whom He prays. He commends to His Father those whom He was about to leave: 'Holy Father, keep through Thine own name those whom Thou hast given Me.'

He speaks as man: 'While I was with them in the world, I kept them in Thy name'; that is, by Thy help. He speaks in condescension to the minds of His disciples, who thought they were more safe in His presence.

The disciples, having heard Him say 'I go to the Father' and being troubled at the thought of it, are taught that to love Him is not to be troubled, but to keep His commandments; this is love, to obey and believe in Him who is loved. And as they had expressed a strong desire for His bodily presence, He assures them that His absence will be supplied to them in another way: He will pray the Father, and the Father will give them another Comforter.

cccReferences.title: Jesus carries His disciples to the Father in His own prayer
On the Cross, prayer and the gift of self are one
The Father's plan of redeeming love inspires Christ's whole life
Christ's words and silences reveal the Father
The Spirit unveils Christ to those who believe

exegesis.thematicConnection: The Lord's word, 'I have conquered the world,' is the very ground on which the Spirit-baptism at Ephesus stands and on which the Psalmist's confidence rests. The disciples who will scatter in the Passion will be re-gathered by the Spirit at Pentecost; the lonely will be brought into a home. The Gospel supplies the source of courage that the other two readings already celebrate as accomplished.

synthesis.title: He has conquered the world, and now He fills it with His Spirit
synthesis.body: Today's Easter weekday readings stand on the edge of Pentecost and proclaim a single mystery: the Risen Christ, having conquered the world, pours out His Holy Spirit to gather a scattered people into His Body. In the Gospel, Jesus tells His disciples that their hour of scattering is upon them, yet He is not alone, for the Father is with Him, and His victory is already won. The Acts of the Apostles shows what that victory looks like in time: in Ephesus, twelve men know only John's baptism of repentance, but Paul completes their preparation by baptizing them into the Lord Jesus and laying hands upon them, and the Spirit descends. What John could only foreshadow, Christ accomplishes; what was scattered in the Passion is now being gathered by the Spirit in the Church. Psalm 68 sings the meaning of it all: God arises, the wicked melt away, the upright rejoice, and the Father of orphans gives the lonely a home. The Church recognizes herself there. Once scattered, abandoned, ignorant even of the Spirit's name, we have been baptized into Christ's death and resurrection, made the dwelling place of the Spirit, and brought into the household of the Father. Therefore the Lord's last word at the Supper becomes our peace today: in the world we will have hardship, but be courageous — He has conquered the world.
